speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number six. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] You-- you were talking about sort of what's been happening to you in your books. Can I just ask you, did you ever look at your diaries?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not open my diaries for 40 years because every word in them was meant for my parents and my parents alone. And I just didn't have the strength or the inclination to open them because I'd be opening my life. Until after that first school reunion in 1985, when I realized that my story was not just my story, but the story of us all, and that I must find the strength and the courage to go back to the diaries and my parents' letters. And once I started writing, I started reliving my life. And things came back to me so vividly. And I felt compelled to go on and on until the book was completed. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Rereading my parents' letters was equally very hard. Particularly translating the letter my mother left for us with Maria, which was given to me in Celakovice after the war, which she wrote on the night before she was taken to the camps, which was on the 1st of January, 1943, on my sister's birthday. And with her usual thoughtfulness, it was a letter to say goodbye, in which she informed me that-- or informed us that father had been taken away by the Gestapo and imprisoned in the [SIGHS] notorious [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] little fortress in Terezin, which was one of the worst prisons. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that she was hoping to see him, that he would be taken to the normal town of Terezin because she managed to bribe the authorities after three months of him being imprisoned in the fortress. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know why he was imprisoned?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was because he, as a Jew, was not allowed to have his own business, and of course, he had a factory. Made an arrangement, as so many did, with an Aryan friend to take it over without any money being passed between them, which, you know, officially it had been sold to him. And some person living in our village had reported them both, and they were both taken to Terezin tower. In fact, the other man died there. And-- but it was the most wonderful letter, not just to say goodbye, but to say how much happiness we had given them, our lives together had given them. How they hoped so much that we would be reunited one day. How they still hoped this might happen. But if it doesn't, you know, she sort of said, be happy. Be brave. Remember us and our lives together, but do not grieve. And we will be reunited one day. And she blessed us, and our husbands to be, and our children. And saying, and ended with the words, we'll be looking down from you from heaven and praying for your happiness. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's a very treasured possession. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Anyway. I'm very glad I wrote my story, and I'm very glad that it's helped so many. I've-- I never realized it would be of interest to people outside the Jewish community, you know, or people of similar sort of backgrounds. But I've got letters from adoption societies, from people who work with refugees, saying how much it helps them in understanding, in their understanding what it means to a child to be separated and-- from their beloved ones, from their homeland. What it means to come to a kind family, loving family. I have letters, for instance, from Czechoslovakia, and a very treasured letter from a lady who wished to die because she was so ill and all alone. And she started listening to the reading of my story because the book was read unabridged there. And she said the courage of the little girl that you were then gave me courage to, you know, go on. And she said each morning as I wake, I look at your dear face-- which is featured on the cover of my book-- and I remember your faith and courage, and I know I can live through yet another day. And then I get letters from children who will say, never again will I take my parents for granted, or gosh, I wish I had a friend like your Marta was. Or, I think I'll be proud for being British, like you were proud for being Czech. You know, that-- things-- and I mean, I-- I'm quoted in synagogues. I'm quoted in churches. And I often give talks in churches and synagogues. And one of my greatest fans is some-- some priest, a Catholic priest in Ireland, whom I've never met. And this goes on and on and on. And every letter is an unexpected bonus. I've got well over 600 of them. And every letter has been answered. And it's, as I said, in talking to schools particularly, gives me great pleasure. I know I'm not a camp survivor. I cannot ever consider my suffering anywhere near their suffering. It's something completely different. But on the other hand, I'm a survivor of our family. And because my experiences weren't as horrific as theirs, and because I grew up in England and led a comparatively normal life, they can identify that much more easily, and they can accept the horror [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as a sideline perhaps more readily than if they were engulfed in it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know what else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything that you might have forgotten earlier that you'd like to add before we finish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, probably 10 million things. I can't think of any at the moment. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yes, another-- one little thing I would like to add. I have never found the strength to go to Auschwitz or Belsen, where my parents had been. They were-- they were both in Auschwitz, anyway. My father wasn't in Belsen. I, of course, have seen it on films. I've read very much about it. But I didn't want to-- I just felt I couldn't face it. I just wanted to carry-- I didn't want to carry the full horror of the place. And I didn't want it in technicolor. I didn't want it with-- with all the trimmings which there exist now. But I did go last year, for the first time. I went to Terezin. I went with Harry. I couldn't have gone alone. That month, the Czech television did a 40 minute documentary on my life. And I was recognized all over the place, and again, it was most rewarding. And when we were in the coach going to Terezin, we had a guide who was a survivor himself, who actually helped to change the Garrison town into a ghetto town. He had to work on it because he was 17 or 18 years old, and he was a work horse. He even had to build the gallows there. But he was wonderful because he gave us the story from the heart. But first of all, he took us to the little fortress. Now, I knew exactly, of course, what happened to my father from my aunt. I knew that he was for two weeks in solitary, in a little hell hole in the ground. And there I saw it. And it really was just a hole in the ground, surrounded by cement, with no light, no room even to sit, never mind stand. And the full horror of it hit me then, and I just broke down. And the guide opened his arms and hugged me, and he said, cry. Tears are good for you. You have to cry. But don't ever forget. Moreover, don't let the others forget. And I said to him, Max-- his name was Max-- that's what I've dedicated the rest of my life to.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said, we'll talk on the way back. And it wasn't until I went to a little kiosk to buy some video that the lady said to me, what are you doing here again? You were here two weeks ago. And I said, no I wasn't. She said, yes you were. I never forgot-- forget a face.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, then it came out that she'd seen my film. So she called Max over, who hadn't seen the film, but he had heard the serialization of my book. And he said he wouldn't have missed a single episode for anything. And you know how rewarding the world is. And so again, this journey to Theresienstadt, though it was so full of grief, was also full of something positive. And it's as if God himself was reminding me that something good has come out of all the suffering that had happened for me personally. And it-- it makes me-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it makes me accept what happened that much more easily. You know, it helps. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there a message or something that you would like to leave [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think all of us who've given testimonies have given them with one thought in mind. Let the world never forget it happened. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It certainly happened. That's got-- got to be ingrained in all people's minds. And the second thought is that they must make sure, each in their small way, nothing's too small a way, to make sure it does not happen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a pleasure. [CHUCKLES] [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's this?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about this photograph, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my maternal grandparents, taken some years before I left for England. So I would say probably in the-- in the 1920s. And my grandmother was already blind then, as you can see. They lived in Prague. My grandfather was a businessman and a musician and a politician.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me their names, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were Matilda and Julius Kestner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father and mother, Karel Diamant and Irma Diamantova, taken after my sister and I came to Britain, just before war started. So it would have been taken in August 1939. The last photograph I have of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [INAUDIBLE]. Who's that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother, Irma Diamant, and my baby sister, Eva, who was three months old at the time. The photograph was taken in Prague in March 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does it say on the photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: It says, wife, I don't like you on this.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't know if you want her now [CHUCKLES] or want her later. [INAUDIBLE]. Oh, sorry. This is a photo of my sister, Eva Diamant, and myself, Vera, taken shortly before our departure to England in Celakovice, our home town in-- in Czechoslovakia. The exact date is June 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photo of my sister, Eva Diamant, my foster sister, Dorothy Rainford, and myself at the bottom, taken in Britain when Eva came to stay with me two days into the war, therefore two-- sorry, two years into the war, about 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And who's this?

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photo of my foster parents, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Gladys Rainford and Robert Rainford, taken in Liverpool in 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my best childhood pal, Marta Pavlickova, taken in 1939, shortly after I left for England, in my home town of Celakovice, with the three kittens, to prove they hadn't been drowned. These photographs are of my cousins, Tomas Kestner on the left and Hanza Kestner on the right, taken in 1939, in Prague. They both perished in buried in Bergen-Belsen after the liberation. This is Marta now. OK, this is a picture of my son's family. He's on the left with his son Christian and wife Cheryl and son Joshua. The picture is taken in his home in Thame in Oxfordshire, about two years ago, so in 1994. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE]. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: On this photograph is on the left, my husband, my late husband, Michael Gessing, with me in the middle, and my aunt Bertha on the right hand side. We are standing in front of what was our home in Celakovice. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The picture is taken in 1968.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, who's this?

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us about this one?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my youngest daughter, Sally Gissing. This photograph has been taken very recently, this year, in August 1996 for a magazine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Called?

speaker: interviewee
text: What's the magazine called? I can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: 1996, August 1996. Perhaps I should say it again. [CHUCKLES]

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] .

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. This is a picture of my English foster mother, Mammy Rainford, or Gladys Rainford, on her 83rd birthday. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Taken in Hemel Hempstead, Hertfordshire. She will be 100 years old this November. That is, November 1996. A lovely face, hasn't she?

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of me, Vera Gissing, and my best friend from my childhood, Marta Pavlickova, taken in Celakovice in 1995, when my book came out in Czech. And I went back to my hometown to give talks and to meet all the townfolk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your book was called?

speaker: interviewee
text: my book was called, and is still called today. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of Harry Steinhauer and myself [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a year ago [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in-- oh god-- in Provence, where we were on holiday. That is 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of the mayor of Llanwrtyd Wells. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] His name is Bryn Jones. We, the children of the school, which was housed in a hotel in Llanwrtyd Wells, brought him the mayoral chain because Llanwrtyd Wells was a village which was suddenly made into the smallest town in England and Wales. And I am there behind him, putting on his mayoral chain in-- in September, 1985.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that name, the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-L-A-N-W-R-T-Y-D Wells. [CHUCKLES]

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] of this complete interview.
